planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Bodinier's beautyberry produces more berries per cluster and larger, more metallic berries than the American species. Plant in groups of three or more for best berry production through cross-pollination. Prune hard (to 6 inches) in late winter each year to maintain compact, floriferous growth.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Bodinier's beautyberry is the Chinese species most widely considered superior to the American beautyberry (Callicarpa americana) for ornamental use, producing extraordinarily vivid, metallic violet-purple berries in very large, densely packed clusters along the arching branches in October–November — the intensity of the color approaches an almost unreal, electric quality that is one of the most striking autumn fruit displays in temperate horticulture. The clusters are larger, more densely packed, and the berry color more metallic and saturated than the American native. The cultivar 'Profusion' is the most widely planted and has become a standard of the British and American garden for its exceptional berry production. Unlike the American species, Bodinier's beautyberry is hardy to zone 5 without dieback. The pink to lilac summer flowers are small and inconspicuous. After frost removes the leaves, the vivid purple berry clusters on the bare, arching branches are an extraordinary sight that persists into winter and is eaten by birds.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in early summer or plant container-grown plants in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; witherod is naturally a swamp and bog-edge shrub that tolerates consistently moist and occasionally wet soils better than most viburnums. It forms multi-stemmed clumps that can be selectively pruned to maintain size.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; witherod is a multi-season native shrub of wet woodland edges, swamp margins, and boggy thickets from Newfoundland to Florida, producing flat-topped clusters of small, white flowers in spring, followed by berries that go through an extraordinary color sequence as they ripen from early summer through fall: green, then pink-salmon, then red, then blue, and finally blue-black, with the multiple colors sometimes present simultaneously on a single cluster. The fully ripe blue-black berries are edible and have been eaten by Indigenous peoples and settlers — they have a sweet, raisin-like flavor, giving rise to the name 'wild raisin.' The fall foliage turns brilliant red to burgundy-red, often among the best fall color of any viburnum. Outstanding for moist sites, rain gardens, and naturalistic plantings where its tolerance of consistently wet soil is invaluable. The fruit is an important wildlife food for migrating birds in fall.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall or scarify and sow in early spring in humus-rich, well-drained, neutral soil in part shade; black bitter-vetch is an unusual shade-tolerant perennial pea that grows naturally in woodland clearings and scrub. It is slow to establish but long-lived once settled. The common name 'black' refers to the seed color and the way the stem turns black when dried.

directions: Sow in humus-rich, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in part shade to full sun; black bitter-vetch is a distinctive European woodland perennial pea, related to spring vetchling (Lathyrus vernus) but producing deep burgundy-purple to magenta-purple pea flowers in May and June that fade to blue with age, creating a two-tone flower display. Like spring vetchling, it grows as a non-climbing, bushy perennial with attractive, divided vetch-like foliage and goes dormant after seeding — disappearing by midsummer. It is more tolerant of dry, alkaline soils than many vetchlings and colonizes woodland edges and scrub in calcareous soils across Europe. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it improves the soil. Unusual and rarely grown in North American gardens despite being an attractive, easy-care shade perennial that fills the early-summer gap in the woodland garden between the spring ephemerals and the midsummer shade perennials. The common name 'black' refers both to the dark seed color and to the black-purple stem color when dried.

planting.note: Direct-sow in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; French honeysuckle is a Mediterranean biennial that is sown in fall, overwinters as a rosette (surviving light freezes to about 15°F), and flowers in spring. In mild climates (zone 7+) it performs reliably as a cool-season cover crop and winter green manure. It fixes nitrogen abundantly and the fragrant flowers are outstanding bee forage.

directions: Direct-sow in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; French honeysuckle is a biennial Mediterranean legume grown both as a cover crop and as an ornamental, producing brilliant crimson-red, intensely fragrant pea flowers in late spring — striking enough that it was a highly fashionable cottage garden flower in 17th and 18th century Europe, prized for its intense sweet fragrance and vivid color. Known as 'sulla' in Italy and the Mediterranean, it is an important forage crop for sheep and cattle in Mediterranean countries. As a cover crop it fixes substantial nitrogen, tolerates alkaline and chalky soils, and provides outstanding early-spring bee forage with its fragrant nectar-rich flowers. The foliage is ornamental, with broadly pinnate, blue-green leaves. It is outstanding for zones 6–9 as a fall-sown winter annual or biennial that flowers in late spring and then is tilled in as green manure before summer vegetables are planted. More ornamental than most cover crops, it can be grown in flower borders as well as vegetable gardens.

planting.note: Inoculate seeds with appropriate Rhizobium bacteria before sowing in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; cicer milkvetch requires the specific inoculant (cowpea group will NOT work) to fix nitrogen effectively. It is slow to establish in year one but becomes very productive in years 2–3. It spreads aggressively by rhizomes and may outcompete other cover crop species in mixed stands.

directions: Direct-sow (properly inoculated) in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; cicer milkvetch is a perennial legume from Europe and western Asia that has been developed as a drought-tolerant, cold-hardy perennial cover crop and forage plant for the northern Great Plains and intermountain West, where its ability to persist for many years without reseeding offers major advantages over annual cover crop legumes. It fixes nitrogen through rhizobial symbiosis, spreads by rhizomes to form dense, weed-suppressing stands, and tolerates drought and alkaline soils that defeat most legume cover crops. The small, cream-white to pale-yellow pea flowers attract bumblebees and native bees. Unlike many legumes that are annual or biennial, cicer milkvetch can persist and actively fix nitrogen for 10–15 years in appropriate conditions. It is used extensively for rangeland improvement, roadside erosion control, and perennial cover in dryland farming systems of the West. The seed requires a specific Rhizobium inoculant strain to achieve effective nitrogen fixation — purchase and apply the correct inoculant at planting.
